title: Are Traditional Wine Clubs Outdated?
summary:
Wine clubs are still a big business for many wineries, but growth has slowed to a trickle. Some are rethinking the entire model.

The post Are Traditional Wine Clubs Outdated? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Key Points

  • Direct-to-consumer wine club growth slowed to 2% in 2024, down from 11% in 2018.
  • 58% of consumers paused at least one recurring subscription service in the past year.
  • Wineries are shifting toward flexible memberships and exclusive releases to boost engagement.

Not long after husband-and-wife duo Chris and Kathryn Hermann founded Double Zero Wines in Carlton, Oregon, in 2015, they started a wine club because, well, they thought they had to.

“We were looking at what other people were doing, especially in the Willamette Valley and California and said, ‘OK, we’ll need a 10% case discount, we’ll ship wine twice a year’—we checked all the boxes,” says co-proprietor and general manager, Kathryn. 

Being part of a winemakers’ collective, the Hermanns don’t have access to the onsite tasting room, which often facilitates community with members. In 2020, as Double Zero settled deeper into the fine wine realm, the Hermanns traded subscriptions for another obligatory model: biannual private allocations. But reserving the best bottles for the biggest spenders likewise felt “icky,” Kathryn says. About six months ago, the couple abandoned clubs altogether in favor of simply putting their wine up for sale on their e-commerce platform. 

“Why have we conceived of these weird ways of buying things in the wine world?” Kathryn says. “If someone loves your wine, they want to go online and buy however much or little they want.”

Wine clubs are still a big business for many wineries, accounting for 25% of sales channels in 2025, per Silicon Valley Bank’s Direct to Consumer Wine Report. But growth in recent years has slowed to a trickle, averaging 2% in 2024, down from 5% the year before and 11% in 2018. Some are rethinking the model—trading biannual bottle shipments for experiential discounts or merchandise. Others have abandoned it for more personalized approaches to fostering loyalty, like splashy, in-person wine release drops.

“People change—the things they want, the way they live,” says Jen Cossey, general manager of the stalwart Brooks Wine in Amity, Oregon. “Even if you have the same members for ten years, you need to evolve because your customers are. If you’re not speaking to everybody, you’ll get left behind.”

Subscription Fatigue and Aging Drinkers

There’s a lot conspiring against wine clubs beyond soaring online shipping costs. U.S. tasting room visits—long the genesis of club memberships—have slipped amid a broader slowdown in alcohol sales. Generational changes are afoot, as wine-loving Baby Boomers age out of loading cases into their trunks and into retirement homes. Some Gen Xers are cutting back on or abstaining from wine altogether for health or financial reasons as they advance through middle age. 

Gen Z, meanwhile, tends to treat wine as an accessory to experiences rather than something to accumulate. It’s also hard to ignore the effects of subscription fatigue in the era of recurring autopayments—for streaming services and meal kits, Substack newsletters, beauty boxes, and coffee. Some 58% of consumers have paused a subscription within the past year, according to Chargebee’s 2025 Global Consumer Insights Report. Not to mention the democratization of wine discovery and access in the online era.

“We have to think more about how people, especially younger people, shop online and discover stuff rather than who’s going to walk in the door of my tasting room, fill out a form, and give me their mailing address,” says Issamu Kamide, cofounder and CEO of Wonderwerk, a natural wine brand in Santa Cruz, California.

From the get go, Wonderwerk leaned into broad distribution and pop-up events, in part because the winery didn’t have a physical space in the early days. Kamide and his cofounder, winemaker Andy Lardy, have spent the past nine years building the wholesale business and making easy-drinking, quality natural wines at scale. They’re methodically evolving Wonderwerk to resemble a coveted consumer good like sneakers rather than an agricultural product deemed valuable through unapproachable bona fides like vintages.  “We want to make it so exciting that people come rushing for it,” says Lardy. 

Capitalizing on consumers’ learned expectation for “new, new, new all the time,” owing to SKU proliferation in the consumer packaged goods (CPG) world, Wonderwerk treats wine releases more like streetwear or album drops, pairing them with live tasting events and thumping DJ sets. They’re always toying with creative ways to engage with their customers, from limited-release labels with up-and-coming artists to an online party portal that will enable consumers to order wines customized to the party they’re throwing. 

As Wonderwerk’s national reach expands, it’s been recruiting likeminded proxies like Jahmahl Pardner, who started Chicago-based wine club Voice Wine. Pardner focuses on low-intervention indie growers and hosts regular tasting events in his garage. A recent satellite party showcasing Wonderwerk’s latest Chicago release sold out of 120 tickets in 24 hours. 

“The event was packed, and we got feedback from him about what people liked, what people were saying,” Kamide says. 

Personalization Over Customization

Increasingly, it seems, loyalty comes with meeting customers where they’re at rather than forcing the same old model on them. For Kathryn at Double Zero, that means being on call for a client seeking a specific vintage of Pinot Noir by a specific date so he can fly with it to his wine club in Switzerland. “That’s how I take care of them.” 

For Brooks, whose 2,500-strong wine club membership largely comprises Millennials and Gen Xers, it means broadening the notion of “membership,” rather than abandoning it altogether. After subscription numbers dropped in consecutive years owing to consumers’ financial and health concerns, the winery debuted Brooks with Benefits, a choose-your-own adventure club riff. Members’ quarterly dues appear as a credit in their accounts, which they can put toward wine shipments, food in the tasting room, events like cooking classes, and merchandise. 

“For people who say, ‘I can’t be part of a wine club because I have too much wine,’ it solves for that,” Cossey says. “You’re not necessarily committing to wine. We’re inviting people to be part of the community in a way that makes sense for them.”

Last summer, the winery also joined forces with seven likeminded, sustainable indie wineries, all from different appellations, to roll out the Atlas Wine Collective. This multistate club share offers perks like complimentary tastings and discounts on wine after identifying members through POS-developed technology. In other words, a member at one is treated like a member at all. The group aims to expand with a bluesky goal of being in every state. 

Indeed, as Kathryn points out, this disconcerting moment has an upside in that it’s forcing wineries to think outside the (subscription) box. 

“We’re in a new era of wine buying; everything has been scrambled,” she says. “A lot of winemakers are scared, but I think this is a tremendous opportunity to change the way we sell and communicate to our customers.”

title: These 12 Chablis Are Pure Cha-Bliss
summary:
Chardonnay is produced around the world, but the varietal arguably shines brightest in Chablis. Here's why.

The post These 12 Chablis Are Pure Cha-Bliss appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Driving into Chablis, the town at the center of Burgundy’s northernmost region, the road descends into what feels like another world. It may be just 10 miles off the A6 highway that runs from Paris to the South of France, but quiet, wine-dominated Chablis is a step back in time.

Gabled houses with slate roofs surround a massive church. Narrow streets are full of the essentials of French country life. Bakers, butchers, and cafés flourish despite the existence of modern supermarkets.

And then, there are the wineries. You find them down every street, where they sit behind discreet doors that lead to interior courtyards and underground cellars. You almost can’t escape the vines—they’re what Chablis lives and breathes.

What Makes Chablis Special?

While not explicitly written on the label, all wines from Chablis are made from 100% Chardonnay. No other grape is allowed in the four Chablis Appellation d’Origine Contrôlée (AOC), and no one has seen the need to change that. It is here, in the northernmost region of Burgundy, that some believe the grape is expressed in its “purest” form.

Like all of Burgundy’s regions, Chablis has a legally enshrined hierarchy based on the quality of the land and soil. The cool-climate region is actually closer to Champagne’s vineyards than southerly sections of Burgundy, which makes vineyard orientation critical. South-, southeast-, or southwest-facing vineyards experience more sun exposure, grow better fruit, and generally make better wines. Proximity to the Serein river also adds a few extra degrees of warmth, which can result in added richness.

There are four levels in the Chablis appellation hierarchy: Petit Chablis, Chablis, Chablis Premier Cru, and Chablis Grand Cru. The latter three are the most lauded, especially where vineyards are planted on Kimmeridgian soil. Named after the English village of Kimmeridge where it was discovered, this chalk soil dates to the Jurassic period, and its composition of fossilized sea creatures gives Chablis its steely or flinty character.

With its cool climate and unique soils, Chablis expresses the terroir with an entirety that’s impossible to find in warmer regions, even its great Burgundian neighbors to the south. In the aging process, oak is sometimes used on higher-end bottles, but most Chablis producers encourage the purity of their fruit with steel-tank fermentation. As a result, tropical, rounded, oaky, or buttery are seldom used as descriptors in Chablis. Instead, these wines are crisp, textured, and acid-driven with electricity and a delicious whisper of fruit. 

For Chardonnay lovers, Chablis is pure heaven (which is why some wine lovers endearingly call it “cha-bliss”). Even if you’re a staunch ABC (anything-but-Chardonnay) drinker, give Chablis a chance to change your mind. This is Chardonnay as you have never had it before.

Grand Cru

Top of the heap in taste and price are the Grand Crus. The seven south-facing vineyards cover about 101 hectares on a steep slope on the right bank of the Serein, looming over the town of Chablis. Although it may be one slope, each Grand Cru vineyard derives its own character from its steepness and exposure. Styles can range from crisp and elegant to fruity and opulent with a balancing minerality. The seven designations are as follows: Blanchot, Bougros, Les Clos, Grenouilles, Preuses, Valmur, Vaudésir. No single producer owns an entire Grand Cru vineyard—just an acre or two here or there—and it’s fascinating to taste the differences, particularly if a producer has vines in different plots.

Grand Cru wines are made to age and should rarely be enjoyed earlier than five years after release. After a decade, they’ll truly start to hit their stride. Keep an eye out for older Grand Crus in a bin sale or at a bargain price on a poorly managed wine list. Or, snag one of our picks below, and stash it in your cellar.

Louis Jadot 2021 Blanchot Grand Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

This wine displays great tension and laser-like precision, featuring notes of white peach, acacia, white mushroom, and wildflowers, all underscored by an abundance of minerality. On the palate, it offers good volume, texture, and acidity, with umami-rich fresh apples, kelp, and lady pink apples. Complex and versatile, it pairs beautifully with spiced dishes and can cellar well through 2035+. Cellar Selection. 95 Points — Anna-Christina Cabrales

$150 Total Wine

Domaine Laroche 2020 Les Blanchots Grand Cru “Reserve de l’Obedience” (Chablis)

For this reviewer, the delicious umami midpalate of this wine is everything. Framed by fresh verbena skin, lemon oil, acacia spice, dandelion, celery leaf and black peppercorn, the wine is exciting now but can benefit from cellaring through at least 2028. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$267 Wine Chateau

Domaine Louis Moreau 2022 Grand Cru Les Clos Chardonnay (Chablis)

Concentrated aromas of biscuit and pastry dough open on the nose along with a touch of sweet baking spice and dried hay. The palate is robust and built around a persistent fruit core that is framed by yogurt and toasted bread on the lingering finish. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$105 Saratoga Wine

Premier Cru

Premier Cru is high in quality but still widely available to consumers. Nearly 800 hectares stretch across the slopes above both banks of the Serein, as well as in some adjacent valleys. There are 40 total vineyards designated as Premier Cru based on their exposition, the slope, and the density of the Kimmeridgian chalk. However, many of their names rarely appear on wine labels.

Some of the smaller Premier Crus will often band together to produce wines under brands with greater recognition, so those 40 vineyards have essentially been reduced to 17 main sites—those are the names you’ll often see on labels. Of course, producers have the choice of using the name of the smaller Premier Cru or the larger, depending on their commercial preferences.

The Premier Cru vineyards on the right bank produce wines that are warm, rich, and powerful. Some of the most well-known vineyards on this bank are Mont de Milieu, Montée de Tonnerre, Fourchaume, and Vaucoupin. On the left bank of the Serein, Premier Crus tend to produce more restrained wines that bring out a flinty character. Vineyards to look for are Côté de Léchet, Vaillons, Montmains, Vosgros, and Vau de Vey. Enjoy most Premier Cru wines five to 10 years after release.

Domaine Billaud-Simon 2022 Montée de Tonnerre Premier Cru (Chablis)

The wine has a lively, jovial nose of white peach, Golden Delicious apple, dried field grass and yellow-pear skin. The palate is reserved, creamy and elongated with a vein of fruit enveloping plush acidity. This wine is substantial and balanced enough to be enjoyed on its own. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$79 Vins Rare

La Chablisienne 2023 Vaillons Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

Alluring aromas of white peach, golden apple and candied ginger skin open on the nose and draw you into the glass. The palate is a plush amalgam of orchard fruit, brioche and honeysuckle with a persistent streak of minerality that runs throughout the length of the taste. Drink now; this wine will only continue to improve with age. Cellar Selection. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$75 Spirited Wine

William Fèvre 2023 Vaillons Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

Crushed stone and green apple mingle on the nose that rests against a wall of baby’s breath and muddled white blossom. The steely palate accentuates purity of fruit while elevating freshness on the finish that is lifted by a nervy layer of acidity. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$105 Wine.com

Domaine Besson 2023 Vaillons Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

This attractive bottling opens with a complex nose of muddled golden apple, wild field herbs, hay and forest floor that gathers momentum the longer you swirl it in your glass. The palate brings feathery, chalky acidity to the fore with minerality that lingers and permeates throughout and encourages the next sip. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$26 Gary’s Wine

Chablis

With over 3,700 hectares of vineyards, the classification most widely available is labeled simply “Chablis”. The wines are fruity yet balanced by a steely, mineral character that epitomizes northerly Chardonnay.

While they’re not particularly well-equipped for aging, the simple Chablis is a joy to drink young—as soon as they’re released. Instant gratification lovers, rejoice!

Domaine Laroche 2022 Vieille Voye Chardonnay (Chablis)

This wine exudes high-toned aromas of delicate wild flowers, acacia and limestone. It is lean on the palate, yet layered with vibrant notes of lemon oil, limestone, and seashells. Its complexity unfolds with each sip, offering refined balance and captivating depth. 91 Points — A.C.C.

$50 Zachy’s

Albert Bichot 2022 Domaine Long-Depaquit Chardonnay (Chablis)

Lilac, white-button mushroom and pastry aromas are accented by orange blossom on the nose. Creamy in texture, orchard fruit on the palate is lifted by a chalk-dusted undercurrent of minerality. Pair with charcuterie, fried fish and au gratin potatoes. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$44 Wine to Ship

Jean-Marc Brocard 2022 Sainte Claire Chardonnay (Chablis)

A reserved mineral-driven nose weaves in bosc pear, yellow apple and forest floor aromas that are well integrated. The relaxed palate is gently textured with lifted orchard fruit that is accented by lemon blossom and wet river stone. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$25 Ultimate Wine & Spirits

Petit Chablis

There’s one other category in the Chablis hierarchy: Petit Chablis, or “little Chablis.” Sitting on the plateau atop the region’s slopes, these Chardonnay vines are planted on Portlandian soil—a different type of chalk soil that also traces back to southern England. It’s a few hundred million years younger than its Kimmeridgian counterpart, and less rich in fossils, but don’t underestimate these wines.

Despite its meek moniker, Petit Chablis is an attractive, light, and fruity style that’s crisp and ready to drink within a few months of harvest. These wines offer an interesting and expressive Chardonnay at an accessible price.

Domaine Passy le Clou 2022 Chardonnay (Petit Chablis)

Fragrant aromas of sun-kissed yellow apple, bread dough and orange zest swirl in the glass accented by a small, bite-sized corner piece of honeyed toast. Rich in texture, the wine is well-balanced with a reserved acidity that complements the fruit core. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$32 Plum Market

J Moreau & Fils 2023 Les Petits Dieux Chardonnay (Petit Chablis)

Layered aromas of green apple, Asian pear and dried lemon peel rest on a bedrock of forest floor. The palate begins with citrus and moves into a chilled minerality that fills and lingers on the finish. Begin an evening with this wine as an aperitif. 90 Points — A.C.C.

$30 Redneck Wine Company

  • Or, read about the making of two 100-point California Chardonnays.

title: Actor Sam Neill Changed New Zealand Wine Forever
summary:
The beloved 'Jurassic Park' actor and New Zealand wine titan has died at 78. We’re sharing our conversation with the winemaker back in 2021.

The post Actor Sam Neill Changed New Zealand Wine Forever appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
On Monday, July 13, Sam Neill’s family announced that the legendary Jurassic Park actor and New Zealand wine titan has died at the age of 78. Neill’s award-winning Pinot Noirs from his Central Otago wine estate, Two Paddocks, helped the rugged, remote wine region achieve global renown. 

“I’m part of the winegrowing community here,” Neill told our Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large Christina Pickard in 2025. “I’m proud to be part of it because in the last 30 to 40 years, we’ve established this extraordinarily tiny region in an obscure part of the world as one of the great Pinot Noir–growing areas.”

To honor his extraordinary legacy, we’re sharing this interview we did with the actor back in 2021.   

Though movie fans may know him for blockbusters like Jurassic Park or indie flicks like Hunt for the Wilderpeople, Sam Neill’s reputation in the wine world hangs on Pinot Noir.

In 1993, he established Two Paddocks winery in Central Otago, New Zealand. Since then, the estate has earned accolades for vibrant, transparent Pinots produced from four distinct vineyards.

We last chatted with Neill back in 2008. With Jurassic World: Dominion now on the horizon, we decided to check in again—this time, via Zoom from his quarantine hotel room in Auckland, New Zealand—to learn more about how 2020’s events altered his perspective on wine.

Have the pandemic and various quarantines changed your perspective on wine?

I’ve been encouraging people to drink less, but better. To really get interested in what’s in the glass, who grew it, what it’s about…  When you’re locked down and putting up with your own company…you might as well be mindful of what you’re doing.

What was it like to film Jurassic World during a pandemic?

That was an unusual experience and one of the toughest I’ve ever had. We were locked up for effectively four to five months, tested every day for 10 days when we arrived, and three times a week after. Someone estimated they spent $5 million just on Covid-19 precautions.

You’ve gushed openly about the magic of Central Otago. Do you feel the same now?

Someone once said, “You can love many places in the world, but you can only be in love with one.” The only place in the world I’m in love with is Central Otago. I get off the plane, and within an hour, I feel completely at home.

You recently produced a small amount of Pinot Noir naturally, without intervention or preservatives like sulfur. Did you find much difference between that and the Pinot you make conventionally?

We made two small batches from exactly the same plot: the Fusilier Vineyard in Bannockburn. One was a natural wine, and one was classic. It was really interesting, the results, because they were completely different wines. They could have been from a different vineyard altogether, but they were from exactly the same grapes.

Did the pandemic disrupt vineyard and cellar work for you?

When harvest hit, it was full-on pandemic, but the government made an exception for the wine industry with very strict protocols in place. Everyone got a dedicated bucket, a dedicated set of secateurs, and people had to keep about 6.5 feet apart all day. We estimate that every vine is visited by a pair of hands 14–15 times a year. It’s time consuming and repetitive work, from leaf plucking to harvest, so it helps when you’re all in it together. To be separate and doing it isn’t so much fun.

What’s your vision for the winery in the next five or 10 years?

Well, all four vineyards were certified organic in 2017. It’s becoming more important to people, and it certainly is to us. There was one vineyard that was problematic that we had to farm conventionally for a while, but we eventually decided everything had to be organic. It’s time consuming and expensive, but I couldn’t in all conscience live with myself if we weren’t farming organically and sustainably.

How do you feel about natural wine, or wine that’s made without anything added or taken away?

We got interested in natural wine, which I laugh a bit about, but there’s demand for it. We make a small amount for our wine club members… I don’t really approve of it, to be honest. How long has mankind been making wine? I suppose it’s 10,000 years or something? And one of the great breakthroughs happened about 3,000 years ago when they started putting in a little bit of sulfur from Mt. Etna in Sicily. They discovered wine could have a life, rather than be a home brew, so why we’re reverting to something your grandad made in his garage. I don’t really know.

What can you tell us about Last Chance, the beautiful windswept vineyard at the end of the world?

If I had to pick one of my vineyards, that would be the one. There are extraordinary, weathered rocks that look like ancient creatures, and green vines growing below them. It has fabulous views looking north into the sun. It seems an unlikely place for a vineyard initially, but it has the full intensity of that Central Otago sun that’s like nowhere else. It’s a bit windier than elsewhere, so it produces tough, small berries and small bunches. Alexandra, the subregion, was always overlooked, but I think we make some of the most interesting, elegant, restrained wines from there. We hold back Last Chance an extra year. It is the slowest to come forward because of these lovely tannins. Next year, we’ll release the 2018.

What’s distinct about this wine? How would you recognize it when tasting it blind?

It’s got lovely savory qualities. It’s the most savory of our wines. It’s surrounded by wild thyme. Thyme even grows between the rows. I’ve always thought that partly accounts for its profile. It also has a subtlety and a kind of reticence that calls for attention, which is exactly what I’ve been talking about, that mindfulness that you need to appreciate Pinot Noir.

content:
Furniture for Wine Lovers: Stylish Pieces with Cooling Storage

By JULIE SPACE

Wine storage has evolved well beyond the cellar. Today’s collectors are bringing their bottles into the heart of the home with thoughtfully designed furniture that pairs climate-controlled preservation with exceptional craftsmanship. From sculptural credenzas to statement bar cabinets, Wine Enthusiast’s furniture with cooling is designed to protect both your wine and your favorite beverages, creating a versatile, mixed-use solution that complements the way you live and entertain.

Since introducing our first integrated cooling design in 2019, these multifunctional pieces have become a hallmark of modern wine living, reflecting a move away from open bar carts and a growing preference for beautifully designed storage that feels as intentional as the collection itself. 

“Customers are spending more time hosting and living with wine in their primary spaces,” says Cameron Jordan, Wine Enthusiast’s Senior Category Manager, Furniture. “They want adaptable pieces that can be focal points in living spaces where the need is not just for storage but also to gather for communal wine enjoyment.”

Inspired by design trends and how we live today, Wine Enthusiast creates a curated selection of beautifully crafted furniture with cooling designs to suit a range of tastes, collections, and spaces. Here’s a look at the options and top-selling styles that bring wine into the social center of your home.

Comparing Storage Styles: Integrated vs. Optional Cooling

When exploring wine furniture, you’ll find two approaches to climate control: integrated and optional cooling. Both styles offer a seamless balance of form and function, and the best fit for your home will depend on your specific hosting and collecting habits, as well as the aesthetics of your space.

Furniture with Integrated Cooling

Furniture that comes with integrated, or built-in, cooling features refrigeration that’s already installed in a pre-set configuration when you order it.

Benefits

  • Arrives fully assembled and ready to use
  • A single outlet powers multiple units, for convenient plug-and-play operation
  • Precision craftsmanship creates a clean, streamlined appearance
  • Classic designs with timeless finishes complement almost any decor
  • Available in pieces with up to four cellars to store larger collections

Standout Selections

Ruskin 2-Door Cabinet With Integrated Wine and Beverage Refrigerators

Perfect for apartments or intimate dining nooks, the compact Ruskin proves you don’t need sprawling square footage to host a full-service bar. Its elegant fluted glass doors softly veil your wine collection and beverage cans, while amber LED lighting glows from within. Verified customer Betty B. says, “Beautiful piece of furniture, very solid. I am so thrilled with this purchase.”

  • Includes one 26-bottle wine fridge and one beverage fridge
  • Crafted from rich walnut veneers
  • Dimensions: 44”W x 40”H x 22”D
Shop Now

Touraine Reeded Bar Cabinet With Integrated Cooling

Commanding attention with its impressive height, the Touraine utilizes vertical space to create a striking focal point rather than consuming precious floor width. Crafted with texture-rich reeded woodwork, it comes equipped with two wine fridges for storing both reds and whites at eye level. “Could not recommend this piece more,” says verified customer Cory S. “It is the anchor to my modern living/wine room.”

  • Includes two 26-bottle wine fridges, one beverage fridge, and one storage cabinet.
  • Crafted from solid oak with a bleached finish and black iron accents
  • Dimensions: 42″W x 23″D x 78″H
Shop Now

Montagny Sideboard With Integrated Wine and Beverage Refrigerators

Montagny’s sleek, low-profile silhouette offers an understated way to anchor a dining room wall without overwhelming it. Balancing concealed cooling zones with a 2-door center display cabinet, its glass-covered service top sits at the perfect counter height for pouring cocktails or serving up a spread to go along with them.

  • Includes one 26-bottle wine fridge, one beverage fridge, and one double-width storage cabinet
  • Crafted from oak veneers with a rubbed black finish
  • Dimensions: 82”W x 40”H x 22”D
Shop Now

Furniture with Optional Cooling

These pieces are made with traditional shelving storage, and feature adaptable compartments that can be outfitted with cooling units in various configurations of your choosing.

Benefits

  • A flexible solution for an ever-evolving collection
  • Cooling units can be added or swapped if your needs change
  • Interior layout can be tailored to match your specific wine, beverage, and storage needs
  • Distinctive looks and functionalities offer statement-making style

Standout Selections

Carousel Acacia Wood Sideboard With Cooling Storage Option

Want a bar that stays discreet until the party starts? The Carousel masquerades as a gorgeous piece of furniture with a seamless wood facade. The magic happens when you slide back the vanishing pocket doors to reveal space for optional fridges, alongside a hidden center cabinet that rotates with a quick push to unveil your spirits. One verified customer, Ron S., shared, “We love the quality, the style and definitely the functionality… the rotating shelf [was] a perfect surprise.”

  • Includes one fixed storage cabinet plus two cabinets that can hold or one or two 28-bottle VinoView® wine cellars, one or two beverage fridges, one of each, or remain as standard shelving
  • Crafted from solid acacia wood with black granite top
  • Dimensions: 66″W x 25″D x 42″H
Shop Now

Aya Wave Wine Credenza With Cooling Storage Option

A sculptural conversation piece, the Aya Wave’s nature-inspired contours bring a sense of organic movement to any space. It also hits the sweet spot for functionality, offering both glass-front doors for display-worthy items or optional fridges, and a hidden 2-door storage cabinet for more utilitarian barware. “Absolutely beautiful piece of furniture,” says verified customer Darby C. “We put the beverage fridge on the right and it is the perfect size. The wavy wood doors are especially pretty.”

  • Includes one fixed double-width storage cabinet plus two cabinets that can hold one or two 20-bottle Evolution wine fridges, one or two beverage fridges, one of each, or remain as standard shelving
  • Crafted from solid acacia wood and veneers
  • Dimensions: 80”W x 35.5”H x 22.5”D
Shop Now

Sonora Oak Mosaic 76” Wine Sideboard With Cooling Storage Option

Handcrafted by artisans in Mexico, the Sonora is the ultimate showstopper. Its facade features a breathtaking mosaic of end-grain oak cuts that form an abstract tree design framed by naturally oxidized metal. Hidden within is generous shelving storage, and the option for varying configurations of stemware and bottle racks or cooling fridges. “We love this wine credenza,” says verified customer Janet B. “It is truly an artisanal type of piece and is very substantial and well crafted.”

  • Includes one fixed double-width storage cabinet plus one cabinet that includes an 18-bottle wine rack and 6-glass stemware rack. Replace the wine rack with an 18-bottle wine fridge, or replace both racks with a 32-bottle Dual Zone MAX cooler
  • Crafted from sustainably sourced oak and naturally oxidized metal with aged patina
  • Dimensions: 80”W x 35.5”H x 22.5”D
Shop Now

Built-In Convenience or Customizable Flexibility? The Bottom Line

Ultimately, the choice hinges on how you want your space to evolve. If you’re looking for an immediate hosting station with high-performance cooling locked into the architecture from day one, an integrated unit offers the ultimate in turnkey luxury. But if you want the freedom to swap between traditional storage and advanced climate control as your collection changes over time, optional cooling delivers unmatched versatility. Whichever path you choose, you’re no longer compromising between an appliance and fine furniture—you’re investing in a centerpiece that masters both.

title: It’s Hard to Find a Bad White Burgundy—These 10 Are Exceptional
summary:
From splurge-worthy Grand Cru to more affordable Pouilly-Fuissé, these white Burgundies express the full range of Chardonnay.

The post It’s Hard to Find a Bad White Burgundy—These 10 Are Exceptional appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The white wines from France’s famed Burgundy region—most often Chardonnay—are some of the most beloved around the world. The region’s cool climate and limestone-rich soil yield exceptional bottles, whether it’s a splurgy Grand Cru for special occasions or an affordable everyday option from one of the larger appellations.

“There’s something about the complex aromas of white Burgundy that you just can’t ignore,” says Anna-Christina Cabrales, Wine Enthusiast’s Tasting Director. “The refreshing combination of elegance or power balanced with a complex layer of florals and minerality tells me I’m in Burgundy. In developed wines, this only intensifies, but the condition of fruits and florals feel dry or dehydrated with interesting notes of toffee or honey.”

White wines from Burgundy are often savored on their own, but they’re especially versatile when it comes to pairing. “Enjoyed with food, I often regret that the bottle wasn’t a magnum,” Cabrales says.

The Wide World of White Burgundy

Though the reputation of “white Burgundy” might already carry weight to the average wine drinker, the many subregions that make up the côtes each bring something unique and impressive to the table.

On the lighter side of the spectrum, Chablis is arguably the most famous subregion, hailed across the globe for its incredibly crisp, mineral-driven profile. Somewhat similar in style are Montagny and Mercurey—perennial value-driven picks that punch well above their price point.

Puligny-Montrachet and Meursault fall on the richer, more layered side, boasting round, sometimes buttery notes with a strong backbone of acidity. Pouilly-Fuissé also offers full-bodied opulence with ample citrus and mineral notes. The power and regality of Corton-Charlemagne never ceases to impress, while up-and-comer and insider darling Saint-Aubin (known to be sharp when young and fuller with age) makes its case for being the next big thing.

While it may be hard to find a bad Chardonnay from Burgundy, we figured we’d help you out by picking 10 bottles that stand out from the already impressive pack. From budget-conscious Meursault and Pouilly-Fuissé to indulgent Premier Cru worth saving for a celebration, look for these bottles now.

Grand Cru

Xavier Monnot 2023 Grand Cru Chardonnay (Corton-Charlemagne)

Ripe citrus and orchard fruit fill the glass, along with fresh almond and a touch of fine vanilla in an airy, understated profile. The palate is round yet finely textured, while its refreshing acidity lifts a delicate orchard-fruit core. Give it an hour of aeration and be ready for it to wow your guests. 97 Points — Anna-Christina Cabrales

$575 JJ Buckley

William Fèvre 2022 Grand Cru Les Preuses (Chablis)

A generous, energetic bouquet captures the freshness of the first day of summer, balancing ripe fruit and elegant florals atop pronounced limestone minerality. The textured palate is lifted by ample acidity, allowing flavors to revisit and linger beautifully. This wine is truly stunning. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — A.C.C.

$150 K&L Wine Merchants

Louis Latour 2022 Grand Cru Chardonnay (Corton-Charlemagne)

A vibrant floral bouquet leads, with freshly peeled red apple skin, toasted almonds, jasmine, and a touch of white mushroom. There’s a gentle roundness on the palate, layered with quiet complexity and a clean, stony finish. Still in its adolescence, this wine hints at depth yet to come—best revisited after 2028 to fully appreciate its unfolding character. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$375 Louis Lator

Premier Cru

Au Pied du Mont Chauve 2022 Les Macherelles Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chassagne-Montrachet)

Fresh and steely on the nose, with freshly squeezed citrus, Bosc pear, white peach and lilac. The palate is driven by mouthwatering acidity, pulling out its saline edge and delicate orchard fruit. An easy win with scallop risotto. 94 Points — A.C.C.

$130 Total Wine

Alex Gambal 2022 Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chassagne-Montrachet)

Clean and powerful on the nose, with citrus oils, peach blossom, and Anjou pear, this wine offers a creamy, lightly buttery note dusted with flaky salt. Subtle Pink Lady apple peeks through, but the terroir steals the show—salty, flinty, and fresh on the finish. Perfect with seafood like octopus, calamari, or oysters. 93 Points — A.C.C

$199 Villa Market

Domaine Chanson 2023 Les Bressandes Premier Cru Chardonnay (Beaune)

Dynamic and expressive on the nose, as if standing on a windswept slope catching glimpses of ripe citrus, orchard fruit, and florals in the breeze. Medium-bodied with ample acidity highlighting saline minerality and ripe citrus and pear, closing on a long trail of white tea and citrus pith. Enjoyable now. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$65 Saratoga Wine

Louis Jadot 2022 Premier Cru (Pouilly-Fuissé)

A vibrant nose bursts with apple skins, tangerine, grapefruit pith, acacia, chamomile, and wet river rock. Racy acidity brings focus to a pulverized stone midpalate and a lingering finish of bright Meyer lemon and subtle clay-river rock minerality. Delightful on its own now, it’s also an excellent match for sushi and other raw fish dishes. 92 Points — A.C.C.

$60 Plum Market

Village Level

Domaine du Cellier Aux Moines 2020 Les Margotons Chardonnay (Mercurey)

Plump yellow apple and lemon curd aromas waft from the glass followed by white rose petal and honeysuckle blossom. Balanced fruit and stony acidity carry the palate that revels in baby’s breath and muddled orange peel. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$40 Viscount Wines

Bader-Mimeur 2022 En Journoblot Chardonnay (Chassagne-Montrachet)

Freshly cut Bosc pear, bergamot, light almond and straw quickly lift right out of the glass. Bright acidity keeps the palate nimble, dancing between raw almond, wet river rock and salinity across the palate. Crisp, refreshing and a perfect way to kick off a hearty meal. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$105 D&M Wine

Domaine des Riottes 2023 Chardonnay (Meursault)

Just ripe Anjou pear, white peach and hay aromatics smell crisp and energetic. The palate is equally lively with good tension and saline minerality. Fine vanilla and delicate florals emerge on the long finish. Refreshing and elegant with shellfish or on its own. 93 Points — A.C.C.

$30 Total Wine

More Chardonnay Coverage

  • Check out our essential guide to Chardonnay.
  • Then, take a deep dive into Chardonnayspeak.
  • Or, read about the making of two 100-point California Chardonnays.
  • If you’re ready to do some more shopping, here are our favorite Chablis at every price point.
  • Want to go stateside? Here are our best California Chardonnays.

title: A Beginner’s Guide to Chianti and Chianti Classico
summary:
Get to know this famed Tuscan wine region through its fascinating history, grapes, and appellations.

The post A Beginner’s Guide to Chianti and Chianti Classico appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Few Italian wines trigger nostalgia in American consumers like Chianti. Many tasted their first sip of tangy Sangiovese from a fiasco, a bottle wrapped in a straw basket, in a red-sauce Italian restaurant. Over the last few decades, Chianti has increased in quality, though most of its top wines fail to titillate palates as seductively as Barolo and Brunello. However, that’s a boon to savvy drinkers that can nab a delicious bottle of Chianti at an attainable price.

Get to know this Tuscan region through its fascinating history, grapes, and appellations.

Chianti from the Romans, the Renaissance, and Today

Tuscany, the romanticized swath of central Italy known for its rolling hills, cypress trees, and stone castles, is also home to Chianti. Its history stems from the Etruscans, who were the first to identify the region as an attractive source for grapes. The Romans further developed the area’s agriculture, which also included olives.

Today, the production zones of Chianti fall around Florence in the north, Siena in the south, Arezzo in the east, and Pisa in the west. These cities’ histories are as rich, complicated, and lurid as a Shakespearean drama.

During the 13th century, acrimony between the Florentine Guelphs and Siennese Ghibellines gave way to the rise of the famous Florentine family, the Medicis, in the 14th century. Savvy merchants and bankers, the Medicis ruled during the Renaissance, when the arts, literature, and wine flourished.

In 1716, Grand Duke Cosimo III de’Medici demarcated the first Chianti wine zone, now known as Chianti Classico. Fast-forward two centuries and production had grown throughout the region. The Italian government created the Chianti Denominazione di Origine Controllata (DOC) in 1967, which was included a central subzone of Chianti Classico.

However, Chianti’s success proved its undoing. In the 1970s, high demand led to a rash of vineyard plantings. Rules that allowed or even required inferior grapes contributed to overproduction and underwhelming wines. Prices and the region’s reputation plummeted, something many producers still battle.

In the late ’70s, a rogue band of quality-minded producers started to bottle wine outside the DOC’s approved grapes, which sparked the creation of super Tuscans. Eventually, Chianti’s rules were modernized to reflect contemporary winemaking and tastes and allowed a certain percentage of these international grapes, but with Sangiovese remaining dominant in the blend.

The appellation would go on to earn Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita (DOCG) status in 1984, Italy’s highest level of wine classification. And in 1996, Chianti Classico separated from Chianti DOCG and became its own DOCG.

Combined, Chianti and Chianti Classico DOCGs continue to grow more wine grapes than any other Italian region save Prosecco, though better clones and a focus on lower yields have lifted quality.

Sangiovese

Sangiovese is Chianti’s heart and hero. Its calling card is mouthwatering acidity, a transparent ruby hue, and flavors of black and red cherry. Further accents of violets, herbs, spice, and earth are common in this dry red. Moderate tannins increase with quality, as does structure and body, which progresses from light to medium. Chianti rarely achieves the body and density of its Sangiovese-based cousin Brunello further south in Montalcino.

Chianti Classico, Chianti, and Its Subzones

Like all Italian wines, Chianti comes with rules. And like all Italian rules, they are frequently confusing. There are several categories of “Chianti.” There’s Chianti, which is the catchall appellation at the bottom of the quality pyramid; Chianti Classico, which has its own appellation; and Chianti Rufina and Chianti Colli Senesi, subzones of Chianti known for their high-quality bottlings.

Chianti DOCG

Since 1996, the rules of Chianti’s broadest appellation require a minimum of 70% Sangiovese and a maximum of 10% being the white grapes Malvasia and Trebbiano. Native red grapes like Canaiolo Nero and Colorino, as well as the international varieties like Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Syrah are also allowed. These add fruit, tannin, or softness to the final blend.

Grapes from across the region (but excluding the Chianti Classico zone) can be blended into the wine. Chianti is meant to be consumed while young, bright, and fresh. Chianti DOCG has two higher-quality categories: Superiore, for wines made from lower yields than straight Chianti, and Riserva, for wines aged at least two years before release.

Chianti DOCG is divided into seven subzones: Chianti Rufina, Chianti Colli Aretini, Chianti Colli Fiorentini, Chianti Colli Senesi, Chianti Colline Pisane, Chianti Montalbano, and Chianti Montespertoli. Wines made in these areas may choose to use the name of their subzone or simply be labeled as Chianti. Of the seven subzones, Rufina and Colli Senesi are the most available in the United States.

Chianti Rufina

Chianti Rufina is considered one of the most quality-driven zones, behind Chianti Classico. Rufina is small by comparison, with production of around three million bottles each year. As a part of Chianti DOCG, Rufina must contain at least 70% Sangiovese, with the remainder blended with Canaiolo, Colorino, or international red varieties.

Rufina is furthest from the coast, and boasts higher-elevation vineyards thanks to its location in the foothills of the Apennine Mountains. Its cooler climate allows slower ripening of Sangiovese. With a substantial difference between day and night temperatures, Rufina retains its acidity and lovely perfume, though wines can be hard and angular without sufficient fruit to bolster them.

Decades ago, during Chianti’s chilliest vintages, elevation could be a curse. But as global climates change, Rufina vineyards may prove to be well positioned.

In style and substance, Chianti Rufina mirrors Classico with its vivid fruit and juicy acidity, along with a tannic structure that lends itself to five to ten years of aging, especially from the best vintages and producers, or along the higher Riserva tier.

Chianti Colli Senesi

After Rufina, Chianti Colli Senesi is the next noteworthy subzone. It takes its name from its location on the hills that envelop Siena in southern Tuscany. Its proximity to the Tuscan DOCGs of Brunello di Montalcino and Vino Nobile di Montepulciano leads to occasional overlap, which adds excitement about Colli Senesi’s prospects of quality.

However, the Colli Senesi designation remains used for Sangiovese-based wines derived from less remarkable sites.

Variation in elevation and soil gives nuance to these Senesi wines, though overall, they tend to be fruit-forward and approachable with a touch of rusticity. New oak and barrique are typically not employed, in favor of purity, spice, and fruit in the wines.

Chianti Classico DOCG

This appellation is located in the heart of the broader Chianti region. The boundaries were first defined in the 18th century, but enlarged significantly in the 1930s. This move was thought by many to have damaged the brand’s reputation, though such expansion is common across Italian wine regions.

Today, Chianti Classico DOCG is considered by many to be the highest-quality offering for Chianti.

The emblem of Chianti Classico is the black rooster, or gallo nero. It relates to a legend told about the use of roosters to settle a border dispute between the warring provinces of Sienna and Florence. The black cockerel was the symbol of Florence, while the white cockerel represented Sienna. It’s clear who dominated that contest.

Marked by refreshing acidity, Chianti Classico DOCG grapes come typically from vineyards planted at higher elevations than Chianti DOCG. Flavors include violet and spice layered atop juicy cherry. Tannins and structure increase with quality, but reflect fruit and terroir rather than oak. New oak, which can slather wine in baking spice and vanilla, has mostly been abandoned. Traditional large oak casks are now preferred, which lend greater transparency to wines.

Chianti Classico must contain at least 80% Sangiovese. A maximum of 20% of other red grapes Colorino, Canaiolo Nero, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Merlot may be used. White grapes were banned in 2006.

There are three quality tiers in the appellation. Annata, or the standard wine, ages for 12 months before release, while Riserva must age 24 months. Gran Selezione has the longest aging requirement at 30 months.

In February 2014, the first top-tier Gran Selezione wines debuted from the 2010 vintage. The category also requires estate-grown grapes and approval from a tasting panel.

Chianti Classico is divided into nine communes. In Siena province: Radda in Chianti, Gaiole in Chianti, Castellina in Chianti, Castelnuovo Berardenga, Poggibonsi. In Florence province: Greve in Chianti, Barberino Val d’Elsa, San Casciano Val di Pesa, Tavernelle Val di Pesa. Though labeling by commune is not yet allowed, wines still reflect distinctions in soil and microclimate, thus further divisions may appear in the future.

title: Chardonnay Is Still King—And These Top-Rated Bottles Prove It
summary:
Whether you keep it old-school with Burgundy or discover a new expression from Oregon, these top-rated bottles have something for everyone.

The post Chardonnay Is Still King—And These Top-Rated Bottles Prove It appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
To the Anything-But-Chardonnay club, we have one word: sorry. In the world of white wine, the varietal still rules—despite decades of bad press around the grape’s defining descriptor being “buttery” and snobs declaring it uncool.

Chardonnay is the most widely planted white grape on the planet, beloved by growers for its adaptability and by winemakers for its versatility. The wine’s regional nuances are vast and varied. Of course, there’s the richer side with vanilla notes courtesy of oak, which put California on the map. Then there’s the crisp, high-acid style achieved by aging in stainless steel, which Chablis has built its reputation around. From France to the United States and beyond, exceptional bottles are produced all over the world. 

To help you get started on your Chardonnay journey (or change some ABCers’ minds), we pulled together the best selections for every palate.

Burgundy

Burgundy is sacred ground for Chardonnay, where white wine is synonymous with the varietal. “When my nose catches an ethereal mix of delicate florals, vibrant citrus—whether lean or rich—and a whisper of minerality, like salt or limestone, I know I’m in Burgundy,” says Anna-Christina Cabrales, Wine Enthusiast’s Tasting Director. “It’s a complete, unmistakable expression—one that’s nearly impossible to replicate.”

Domaine Louis Moreau 2022 Grand Cru Clos des Hospices Chardonnay (Chablis)

Grand Cru Chablis is an experience, and this bottling is no exception. Entrancing aromas of brioche, pastry dough and Empire apple open on the nose that sees a touch of honeysuckle and forest floor. The palate is sinewy muscular in texture with restrained flavors in comparison to the nose. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — Anna-Christina Cabrales

$165 Wine.com

Joseph Drouhin 2023 Clos des Mouches Premier Cru Chardonnay (Beaune)

There is a beautiful intensity to this wine that makes you want to chase it in its youth. A perfumed nose of citrus oil, white peach, acacia, and peony leads into a rich and seamless palate of ripe citrus, salted butter, tight oak spice, and bergamot. Approachable now but should cellar well through 2035+. 96 Points — A.C.C.

Inquire with the importer

La Chablisienne 2022 Fourchaume Premier Cru Chardonnay (Chablis)

Bright citrus aromas with a mineral edge frame the nose with energy. The palate is taut and zesty, acidity sharpening limestone minerality that cuts cleanly across citrus pith. Textured and energetic, it lingers with refreshing precision. Equally suited for enjoying on its own or paired with fresh seafood like oysters, it thrives on vibrancy and clarity. 91 Points — A.C.C.

$83 Solano Cellars

New Zealand

While New Zealand has become renowned for perfecting Sauvignon Blanc, the region has been steadily applying the same rigor to Chardonnay.

“New Zealand has done a lot of growing up in the last decade or so when it comes to Chardonnay,” says Christina Pickard, a Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large who reviews wines from the region. “Better clones, better rootstock, and more knowledgeable growers and makers have raised the quality bar hugely.”

Now in its phase of experimentation, New Zealand has yet to define a singular Chardonnay style, Pickard notes. “Kiwi Chardy will, perhaps, always live in Sauvignon Blanc’s shadow; and that’s a crying shame,” she says. “But start by sipping from Chardonnay champions like those recommended below and you’ll no doubt be tempted to explore this underrated wine style more.”

Dog Point Vineyard 2023 Chardonnay (Marlborough)

This renowned producer is known for powerful, reductive white wine styles, and the ’23 Chardy is no exception. Sure, there’s prominent flinty, matchstick characters from first sniff, but they quickly mingle with roast nut, ground ginger and somewhat brighter citrus and mango rind. Combined, it’s a layered, intriguing start. The acidity lifts and sculpts the palate, countering the textural richness and viscosity, and lending tang to the nutty, flinty finish. Still young (but absolutely drinkable now), it should evoke with flare over the next decade or more. 95 Points — Christina Pickard

$47 Wine.com

Felton Road 2022 Bannockburn Chardonnay (Central Otago)

Softly spoken on the nose with flint and subtle lemon and nougat aromas opening; something a bit floral and herbal waiting in the wings. It really opens up with aeration in glass to something quite pretty, even delicate. The richness on the palate is lifted by a lovely ping of acidity and tingly citrus pith, the spice and toast nuances following. Food friendly and youthful. 94 Points — C.P.

$45 Wine Chateau

Kumeu River 2024 Maté’s Vineyard Chardonnay (Kumeu)

The vines now at 35 years of age, Mate’s is queen of the Kumeu castle, reigning with seamless grace, quiet power and intensity, her crown jewels kept close to her chest, for now, to reveal them slowly over time. While a complete and beautifully made wine now—all coiled acidity, orchard fruit and lightly salted cashew characters amidst a slippery texture— just think of the rewards for those who wait. 98 Points — C.P.

$95 Zachy’s

Australia

Pickard, who also reviews wines from Australia, calls Chardonnay from Down Under “wildly underrated.”

“I’ve said it once—OK, many times—and I’ll say it again—probably many more times—Australian Chardonnay is hands down some of the best in the world,” she says, adding that winemakers in the country “have decoded Chardonnay’s mysteries, striking a balance between bright acidity, salt-flecked vibrant fruit, and toasty, flinty richness.

Of all the regions growing Chardonnay, “Margaret River in particular, with its dominant and distinctive ‘Gin Gin’ clone, proximity to the Indian Ocean, and moderate temperatures, shines when it comes to this variety,” she adds. 

Additionally, these bottles are food-friendly and wind up on Pickard’s dinner table frequently.

Vasse Felix 2023 Heytesbury Chardonnay (Margaret River)

From an excellent vintage and from the distinctive “Gingin” clone, Vasse’s top Chardy is an opulent but extremely well woven wine that drinks beautifully now but could cellar for decades. Roast nuts, flint and beach shells are at the fore, alongside subtle salted citrus and melon. There’s weight, power, texture to the palate, with length and poise. An excellent example of this world class Western Aussie style. 96 Points — C.P.

$106 Mr. D Wine Merchant

Giant Steps 2024 Circle of Fifths Chardonnay (Yarra Valley)

A new label that combines fruit from all single vineyard sites. It’s tightly wound—even a tad austere—but there’s depth and expression of all sites, while also walking to its own distinctive beat. Waxy lemon and seashells accompany a creamier, more textural palate. A through line of bright, oh-so-Yarra Valley acidity shines. This should age beautifully—likely for several decades—but it’s a classy, food friendly drop now with aeration in glass or decanter first. 95 Points — C.P.

$55 Plum Market

Paringa Estate 2024 Peninsula Chardonnay (Mornington Peninsula)

Bright creamy lemony vibes open this maritime-influenced bottling from Victoria’s Mornington Peninsula. There’re notes of green melon and a toasty underbelly. Lovely high toned acidity on the palate instantly gets the mouth watering and the tongue tingling. Subtle creamy nuances add textural weight and food friendliness, leading to a long finish. A delightful modern Aussie Chardy. 94 Points — C.P.

$40 Wine Exchange

California

California Chardonnays are often thought of as big, bold oak bombs in a glass. But these full-bodied, buttery, and vanilla-perfumed bottles are just one slice of the pie. 

“While opulent, heavily oaked Chardonnays helped put California on the map, that’s not the only style that works in the Golden State,” says Tom Capo, a Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large and Californian wine reviewer. “These exceptional examples balance sunshine and depth with dazzling acidity that lifts the wines, as well as making them far more ageworthy and food friendly.”

Flowers 2024 Chardonnay (Sonoma Coast)

Zesty lemon, lime, and green herb aromas mingle with citrus blossoms and cinnamon on the nose of this Chardonnay. The palate delivers a surge of vibrant acidity and pithy texture, with flavors of green apple, Meyer lemon, toasted hazelnut, and chopped lemon balm echoing for over a minute. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — Tom R. Capo

$45 Wine.com

Smith-Madrone 2021 Estate Chardonnay (Spring Mountain District)

Deftly balancing enticing flavor with structure and backbone, Smith-Madrone Chardonnay showcases the allure and verve of the variety. Lemon curd lightly spiced, mixed zest, a squeeze of pomelo and pineapple race through the palate carrying a kiss of butterscotch in the finish. 95 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

$43 Artisan Wine & Spirits

Samuel Louis Smith 2024 Spear Vineyard Chardonnay (Sta. Rita Hills)

The subtlety of this Chardonnay ensures long aging potential, as the delicate notes will evolve in harmony together. Aromas of chalk, cold marble and Asian pear recall a silky white slip on the nose. A tongue-tantalizing brightness inhibits the palate, where flashes of citrus and stonefruit ride an energetic, texturally taut frame. Drink now-2040. Cellar Selection. 96 Points — Matt Kettmann

$55 Flatiron Wines

Signal Ridge 2023 Signal Ridge Vineyard Chardonnay (Mendocino Ridge)

We are at the top of the Mendocino Ridge Coast, the aromatics are heady, ethereal, with windswept, stone fruit, citrus zest, savory herbs, and the saltiness of the sea air on the nose. Vibrant, alpine, saline, dried herbs, Gaja apple skin, White nectarine, Meyer lemon on the palate. You can enjoy it now; it has lots of life. Expressive with expansive length. Cellar Selection. 97 Points — Tonya Pitts

$65 Signal Ridge

Oregon

In Oregon, Chardonnay is much lighter and zestier than in its neighbor to the south. Bottles from the state usually feature balanced acidity, crisp fruit, and linear profiles with very subtle oak. 

Over the past couple of decades, producers in the Willamette Valley have been making increasingly “racy, minerally wines on a high wire at lower Brix and pH levels than we had done previously,” David Adelsheim, founder of Adelsheim Vineyard, told Wine Enthusiast.

“Chardonnay, both in still and sparkling wines, is a growth industry in the Willamette Valley,” says Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large Michael Alberty. “The wine world has devoted a lot of attention to these wines, and rightfully so. Whether you are looking for a taut style framed by electrifying acidity or a slightly richer, fuller style, there’s something here for everyone to enjoy—and argue over.”

Walter Scott 2023 X Novo Vineyard Chardonnay (Eola-Amity Hills)

Rapidly becoming one of the Willamette Valley’s iconic vineyards, X Novo delivers once again. It begins with an undeniable aromatic blend of chamomile tea, buttered popcorn, and tart lemon curd. Blazing acidity then lights up the wine’s flavors of lemon grass, Thai basil and grilled pineapple. Pair this wine with celebrations, such as weddings, mid-term elections, and the last kid out of the house. Editors’ Choice. 96 Points — Michael Alberty

$95 Wine Solutions

Ponzi 2023 Laurelwood Chardonnay (Laurelwood District)

The Laurelwood, a blend of fruit from Avellana, Aurora, Issimo and Paloma Vineyards, is a Chardonnay that will take your breath away with its live-wire acidity and fresh mouthfeel. After an aroma set that includes smoky flint, passion fruit, laurel leaves and a spicy touch of ginger, the Laurelwood lights up the plate with flavors of lime zest, lemon saltwater taffy and tangy gin botanicals like juniper berries and cardamom. Only pour this wine for people that you really, really like. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — M.A.

$40 Ponzi Vineyards

Ambar 2023 Lustral Chardonnay (Dundee Hills)

With all apologies to Iggy Pop, I have a Lustral for life after tasting this compelling Chardonnay. The wine’s decadent apple aroma is almost applesauce-like, with its mild sweetness counterpointed by notes of saline and toasted filberts. Bracing acidity that will make your lips quiver brightens up Lustral’s lime, peach butter and water cracker flavors. For the second year in a row, this is a stunning wine. Editors’ Choice. 95 Points — M.A.

$95 Saison Cellar

Washington

Move farther north to Washington and the cool climate yields Chardonnays that are even lighter and crisper with the ideal combination of “ripeness, fruit, acidity, and structure,” according to Alberty. Green apple, lemon, and other tart fruits reign supreme, but flavors and aromas can vary widely, from chamomile tea to toasted hazelnuts to tangerines. High elevation and cool nights produce acidity that is elegant yet potent enough “to trigger goosebumps.”

“Washington state may not be the first thing that comes to mind when talking about Chardonnay,” Alberty says, “but that would be a mistake.”

Pursued by Bear 2024 Twin Bear Chardonnay (Yakima Valley)

Appropriately enough, given the fact that bears are involved, this Chardonnay is what I call a “Goldilocks wine,” where everything is just right. Twin Bear leads with a rich papaya aroma, complemented by equally tropical notes of Macadamia nuts and Thai basil. The wine’s honeydew melon, lemon verbena tea, and butter flavors glide over a rich, pleasing texture, backed by its gentle acidity: porridge down, Chardonnay fans. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — M.A.

$45 Pursued by Bear

Passing Time 2024 Chardonnay (Columbia Valley)

I’m back in my grandmother’s kitchen thank to this Chardonnay’s lemon curd with a butter crust aroma. With a bit of air time, the wine picks up mild scents of smoky flint and ripe nectarines. There’s a significant amount of new French oak at play here, and you can feel it as the wine’s texture thickens in most pleasing fashion. Flavors of yellow apples, sweet donut peaches and chamomile tea seem to glide across the palate. For Chardonnay fans who prefer their acidity to be of the non-blazing variety, Passing Time will become a favorite. Editors’ Choice. 94 Points — M.A.

$50 Passing Time

Rasa 2023 Dream Deferred Chardonnay (Wahluke Slope)

The floral and earthy scent of bee pollen buzzes about as aromas of pears, warm challah bread and toasted filberts fill the air. The rich palate showcases flavors like lime sorbet and meadowfoam honey, along with a burst of lemon and cinnamon. This is not a dream deferred if you are dreaming of finding a sumptuous Chardonnay that possesses bright, shimmering acidity that is downright jangly. 94 Points — M.A.

$60 Rasa Vineyards

title: This Charming City in Champagne Is a Winemaker-Loved Culinary Hotspot
summary:
Quieter than the more-famous Reims, Épernay has an eclectic food and drink scene that the region’s winemakers love.

The post This Charming City in Champagne Is a Winemaker-Loved Culinary Hotspot appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Champagne’s identity has long been split between two cities: Reims and Épernay. Reims has the grand Gothic squares and urban edge, while Épernay is quieter, shaped by vineyard slopes and the Marne River.

In Épernay, the connection to the land behind some of the world’s most coveted wines feels far more direct. A patchwork of vineyards appears almost as soon as you exit the train station, and some of Champagne’s most celebrated wine villages sit within just a few miles of town—close enough to reach easily by bicycle.

For anyone who has returned to Épernay regularly over the past five years, the city’s transformation is hard to miss. Not so long ago, a walk along the chalk-white Avenue de Champagne—which forms part of Champagne’s UNESCO-listed landscape—could feel surprisingly sleepy. Today, the doors of its prestigious Champagne houses are open wide, with each looking for new ways to impress visitors.

On that same avenue is the recently opened Musée du Vin de Champagne et d’Archéologie Régionale, set inside the eclectic former mansion of the Perrier-Jouët family. The modern, interactive museum offers a concise way to trace 2,000 years of regional history while also unpacking the technical side of Champagne production.

Épernay is gaining new energy, with ambitious restaurants, wine bars, and four- and five-star hotels opening year after year. Champagne, of course, is not a single style but many: chalky and electric from Avize, generous and red-fruited from Bouzy, vinous and earthy from Les Riceys. That range calls for an equally eclectic food scene. As the winemakers who know Épernay best make clear, there is no shortage of places to experience it—from an apéritif before dinner to a long meal after touring the historic chalk cellars.

Where to Eat in Épernay

The town has its share of beloved dining institutions, and La Grillade Gourmande is one of them. Lyon-born chef Christophe Bernard, who worked with the legendary Paul Bocuse, moves from table to table with his servers, presenting signature dishes such as pigeon with foie gras in puff pastry, venison in pepper sauce, and sea bass with fennel. Everything is cooked in the homey dining room, over an open wood-burning fireplace.

“For me, this is Épernay’s standout bistronomic restaurant. I love the wood-fired cooking, which brings tremendous character to the dishes, as well as the chef’s distinctive personality that comes through on every plate,” says Sacha Geoffroy of Champagne Geoffroy, based in Aÿ.

Her father, Jean-Baptiste Geoffroy, once told me about Chez Max, another lively institution on the banks of the Marne that has been welcoming guests for nearly 80 years. At nearby tables, locals from Champagne recall coming here as children and now return with children of their own. Franck Benard and his wife deliver classic French service with real flair, turning tableside steak tartare into a small performance and theatrically presenting a platter of local cheeses. The cooking is generous, built around seasonal ingredients and deeply flavored sauces. If you see the escargot pie on the menu, don’t hesitate.

Before turning to the city’s more cosmopolitan side, two more addresses for classic French cooking come recommended by Odilon de Varine, cellar master at Champagne Gosset, based in Épernay.

“L’Oben is a very recent addition to Épernay and remains something of a hidden address,” he says. “With only a handful of tables, it offers an intimate atmosphere and serves classic French cuisine executed with great finesse.” Think crisp tarte fine with Bouzy Grand Cru snails, followed by farm-raised pork so tender it spent eight hours getting there.

For a more refined dining experience, de Varine recommends Why Not, especially before an evening at the theater. “The restaurant works with regional ingredients but approaches them with the precision and ambition of a gastronomic restaurant,” he says. The room pairs warm wood, high ceilings, muted tones, and low lighting, while the menu leans into French comfort dishes: think aged Comté ravioli gratin and half-roasted Camembert with Marc de Champagne to start, followed by knife-cut Charolais beef tartare.

A break from the classics comes at the hip, locals-loved Sacré Bistro, where the menu includes a signature burger with melted Comté, alongside other seasonal comfort plates. “The wine list there is one of the most interesting in the region,” says Geoffroy. “You’ll find everything from outstanding Champagne gems to rarer wines from around the world that can be difficult to find elsewhere.”

She also recommends stopping by Cook’in, where chef Guillaume Rousseau brings together French aesthetics and Thai flavors, with dishes such as duck foie gras paired with green apple and ginger confit, and grilled beef tournedos with red curry sauce. “It’s a rather unique address in Épernay,” Geoffroy says. “The Thai cuisine is consistently well-executed, and the contrast between these vibrant Asian flavors and the carefully curated Champagne selection works remarkably well.”

The Asian thread continues at L’Eurasienne, a family-run restaurant where bò lúc lắc is served alongside excellent pizzas. It is a particular favorite of Étienne Calsac, a grower-producer based in Avize. “What I appreciate most is that the wine selection is thoughtful and effective, offering good choices without being overly complicated,” he says. Think bún with lemongrass-grilled pork, or a pizza topped with white asparagus, and 24-month-aged Parmigiano Reggiano.

Where to Drink in Épernay

On Avenue de Champagne, one address stands apart from the polished tasting lounges of the Champagne houses. At Bar Brut, a small family-run bar, you won’t find the Grandes Marques. Instead, the focus is on grower friends such as Vincent Charlot, Louise Brison, and Franck Pascal, whose stories the team is happy to share while filling your glass to the brim. Take a seat in the carefully tended inner garden and, after paying Paris prices, prepare to get emotional: a glass of 2017 vintage blanc de blancs costs €7.50.

For an evening drink, Calsac recommends Le Pif Bar. The atmosphere is pleasingly countercultural, with barrels in place of tables and darts played over a glass of Champagne. “It’s a relatively new wine bar that opens only at night and has quickly become part of the local wine scene,” he says.

When Geoffroy meets friends for a glass, she often chooses 75cl, right in the heart of Épernay. “I really like this place for its dual identity as both a wine shop and a wine bar,” she says. “The selection is always thoughtfully curated, with excellent Champagne producers alongside strong choices from other regions. The atmosphere is simple, warm, and welcoming.”

title: Are Traditional Wine Clubs Outdated?
summary:
Wine clubs are still a big business for many wineries, but growth has slowed to a trickle. Some are rethinking the entire model.

The post Are Traditional Wine Clubs Outdated? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Key Points

  • Direct-to-consumer wine club growth slowed to 2% in 2024, down from 11% in 2018.
  • 58% of consumers paused at least one recurring subscription service in the past year.
  • Wineries are shifting toward flexible memberships and exclusive releases to boost engagement.

Not long after husband-and-wife duo Chris and Kathryn Hermann founded Double Zero Wines in Carlton, Oregon, in 2015, they started a wine club because, well, they thought they had to.

“We were looking at what other people were doing, especially in the Willamette Valley and California and said, ‘OK, we’ll need a 10% case discount, we’ll ship wine twice a year’—we checked all the boxes,” says co-proprietor and general manager, Kathryn. 

Being part of a winemakers’ collective, the Hermanns don’t have access to the onsite tasting room, which often facilitates community with members. In 2020, as Double Zero settled deeper into the fine wine realm, the Hermanns traded subscriptions for another obligatory model: biannual private allocations. But reserving the best bottles for the biggest spenders likewise felt “icky,” Kathryn says. About six months ago, the couple abandoned clubs altogether in favor of simply putting their wine up for sale on their e-commerce platform. 

“Why have we conceived of these weird ways of buying things in the wine world?” Kathryn says. “If someone loves your wine, they want to go online and buy however much or little they want.”

Wine clubs are still a big business for many wineries, accounting for 25% of sales channels in 2025, per Silicon Valley Bank’s Direct to Consumer Wine Report. But growth in recent years has slowed to a trickle, averaging 2% in 2024, down from 5% the year before and 11% in 2018. Some are rethinking the model—trading biannual bottle shipments for experiential discounts or merchandise. Others have abandoned it for more personalized approaches to fostering loyalty, like splashy, in-person wine release drops.

“People change—the things they want, the way they live,” says Jen Cossey, general manager of the stalwart Brooks Wine in Amity, Oregon. “Even if you have the same members for ten years, you need to evolve because your customers are. If you’re not speaking to everybody, you’ll get left behind.”

Subscription Fatigue and Aging Drinkers

There’s a lot conspiring against wine clubs beyond soaring online shipping costs. U.S. tasting room visits—long the genesis of club memberships—have slipped amid a broader slowdown in alcohol sales. Generational changes are afoot, as wine-loving Baby Boomers age out of loading cases into their trunks and into retirement homes. Some Gen Xers are cutting back on or abstaining from wine altogether for health or financial reasons as they advance through middle age. 

Gen Z, meanwhile, tends to treat wine as an accessory to experiences rather than something to accumulate. It’s also hard to ignore the effects of subscription fatigue in the era of recurring autopayments—for streaming services and meal kits, Substack newsletters, beauty boxes, and coffee. Some 58% of consumers have paused a subscription within the past year, according to Chargebee’s 2025 Global Consumer Insights Report. Not to mention the democratization of wine discovery and access in the online era.

“We have to think more about how people, especially younger people, shop online and discover stuff rather than who’s going to walk in the door of my tasting room, fill out a form, and give me their mailing address,” says Issamu Kamide, cofounder and CEO of Wonderwerk, a natural wine brand in Santa Cruz, California.

From the get go, Wonderwerk leaned into broad distribution and pop-up events, in part because the winery didn’t have a physical space in the early days. Kamide and his cofounder, winemaker Andy Lardy, have spent the past nine years building the wholesale business and making easy-drinking, quality natural wines at scale. They’re methodically evolving Wonderwerk to resemble a coveted consumer good like sneakers rather than an agricultural product deemed valuable through unapproachable bona fides like vintages.  “We want to make it so exciting that people come rushing for it,” says Lardy. 

Capitalizing on consumers’ learned expectation for “new, new, new all the time,” owing to SKU proliferation in the consumer packaged goods (CPG) world, Wonderwerk treats wine releases more like streetwear or album drops, pairing them with live tasting events and thumping DJ sets. They’re always toying with creative ways to engage with their customers, from limited-release labels with up-and-coming artists to an online party portal that will enable consumers to order wines customized to the party they’re throwing. 

As Wonderwerk’s national reach expands, it’s been recruiting like-minded proxies like Jahmahl Pardner, who started Chicago-based wine club Voice Wine. Pardner focuses on low-intervention indie growers and hosts regular tasting events in his garage. A recent satellite party showcasing Wonderwerk’s latest Chicago release sold out of 120 tickets in 24 hours. 

“The event was packed, and we got feedback from him about what people liked, what people were saying,” Kamide says. 

Personalization Over Customization

Increasingly, it seems, loyalty comes with meeting customers where they’re at rather than forcing the same old model on them. For Kathryn at Double Zero, that means being on call for a client seeking a specific vintage of Pinot Noir by a specific date so he can fly with it to his wine club in Switzerland. “That’s how I take care of them.” 

For Brooks, whose 2,500-strong wine club membership largely comprises Millennials and Gen Xers, it means broadening the notion of “membership,” rather than abandoning it altogether. After subscription numbers dropped in consecutive years owing to consumers’ financial and health concerns, the winery debuted Brooks with Benefits, a choose-your-own adventure club riff. Members’ quarterly dues appear as a credit in their accounts, which they can put toward wine shipments, food in the tasting room, events like cooking classes, and merchandise. 

“For people who say, ‘I can’t be part of a wine club because I have too much wine,’ it solves for that,” Cossey says. “You’re not necessarily committing to wine. We’re inviting people to be part of the community in a way that makes sense for them.”

Last summer, the winery also joined forces with seven likeminded, sustainable indie wineries, all from different appellations, to roll out the Atlas Wine Collective. This multistate club share offers perks like complimentary tastings and discounts on wine after identifying members through POS-developed technology. In other words, a member at one is treated like a member at all. The group aims to expand with a bluesky goal of being in every state. 

Indeed, as Kathryn points out, this disconcerting moment has an upside in that it’s forcing wineries to think outside the (subscription) box. 

“We’re in a new era of wine buying; everything has been scrambled,” she says. “A lot of winemakers are scared, but I think this is a tremendous opportunity to change the way we sell and communicate to our customers.”
